title: Blame It On Lisa

jokes.text: D'oh!
jokes.punchline: Homer's signature exclamation
jokes.explanation: Iconic catchphrase with perfect character integration and economy, though not original at this point in the series

jokes.text: Nice follow-through!
jokes.setup: Golf commentary in cartoon violence context
jokes.punchline: Treating brutal violence with sports commentary politeness
jokes.explanation: Clever juxtaposition of golf terminology with cartoon violence, well-structured dark humor

jokes.text: Now that's what I call a moon shot.
jokes.punchline: Double meaning of 'moon shot' in violent cartoon context
jokes.explanation: Solid follow-up to the golf theme but slightly less clever than the setup

jokes.text: Dad, that line was in the cartoon. I'm pretty sure it wasn't.
jokes.punchline: Homer's confident denial of obvious truth
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer characterization - his stubborn denial of reality is classic

jokes.text: Boy, did you make a prank call to Brazil? No, sir. I didn't. Choke on your lies!
jokes.punchline: Homer's immediate violent escalation despite Bart's honest denial
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer parenting - jumping to violence despite lack of evidence

jokes.text: Which phone company? There are hundreds of them. And they all keep changing their names.
jokes.setup: Simple plan to call phone company
jokes.punchline: Reality of telecom industry confusion
jokes.explanation: Sharp observational humor about telecom industry that aged well

jokes.text: I think we're with Comquaaq. No, I think its Niagular. No, last week they became Vertiqual.
jokes.punchline: Increasingly ridiculous made-up telecom names
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalating parody of telecom merger mania with perfectly absurd names

jokes.text: Wow. Everything is so high-tech. Dialing Murray Hill, 5-6-3-0-9. So, I says to Myrna, I says, 'That guy's a bum!'
jokes.setup: Homer impressed by high-tech phone company
jokes.punchline: Old-fashioned operator with 1950s dialogue
jokes.explanation: Good ironic contrast between 'high-tech' and obviously outdated system

jokes.text: Thank you for dating Mr. Movie Phone. Our evening will be rated R, for brief nudity.
jokes.punchline: Treating phone call like a romantic date with rating system
jokes.explanation: Clever anthropomorphization of automated service with movie rating twist

jokes.text: I'm a sexual predator.
jokes.setup: Question about why she keeps changing jobs
jokes.punchline: Casual admission of being a sexual predator
jokes.explanation: Shockingly dark humor delivered with perfect deadpan timing

jokes.text: Fine! I'll cut off your ponytail! Homer! Marge, it's called negotiating.
jokes.setup: Phone company threatens to cut off service
jokes.punchline: Marge threatens physical violence, Homer calls it negotiation
jokes.explanation: Good role reversal with Marge being aggressive and Homer defending it

jokes.text: Why must you fight with every utility? I told you, I have too much time on my hands.
jokes.punchline: Homer's honest self-assessment
jokes.explanation: Surprisingly honest moment of self-awareness from Homer

jokes.text: We tried, but a recording said you were a bunch of deadbeats.
jokes.setup: Explaining why they didn't call Homer about free taco shells
jokes.punchline: Phone company's insulting outgoing message
jokes.explanation: Solid callback to the phone service cutoff with added humiliation

jokes.text: That's it! They have awoken a sleeping giant. Crazy scheme, crazy scheme, crazy scheme. Get me tools and beer! Yes!
jokes.punchline: Dramatic declaration followed by nonsensical planning process
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - grandiose rhetoric followed by childish planning method

jokes.text: Let's try the red one... Let's try them together... How did I get here?
jokes.setup: Homer trying to restore phone service by cutting wires
jokes.punchline: Montage ending with Homer confused about his location
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer incompetence escalating to absurd consequences

jokes.text: Let's try the red one again. Oh, not again!
jokes.punchline: Repeated electrocution
jokes.explanation: Standard slapstick - functional but predictable

jokes.text: The one I didn't make and Marge didn't make and Bart didn't make and hence, no one in the house made. Uh-oh.
jokes.punchline: Lisa's guilty 'uh-oh' revealing she made the call
jokes.explanation: Well-structured reveal with good misdirection and character surprise

jokes.text: Don't you know the boys from Brazil are little Hitlers? I saw it in a movie, whose name I can't remember!
jokes.punchline: Homer's paranoid movie reference he can't quite remember
jokes.explanation: Good reference to 'The Boys from Brazil' with typical Homer incomplete knowledge

jokes.text: Well, then they started pressuring me for more donations, and you can't hang up on a nun. That's right. They have powers.
jokes.punchline: Homer's belief that nuns have supernatural powers
jokes.explanation: Funny escalation from social politeness to supernatural belief

jokes.text: Aw! He's so adorable. Can we have another baby? No way! I still haven't lost the weight I put on from the last one.
jokes.setup: Marge's maternal response to cute video
jokes.punchline: Homer's blunt rejection referencing Marge's weight
jokes.explanation: Typical married couple humor but somewhat mean-spirited and predictable

jokes.text: Now the monkeys cannot bite me. I am like sugar to them.
jokes.punchline: Poetic description of being irresistible to monkeys
jokes.explanation: Beautifully absurd metaphor delivered with innocent sincerity

jokes.text: How many people live in Brazil? A hundred and fifty-six million. D'oh!
jokes.setup: Deciding to find one boy in Brazil
jokes.punchline: Homer realizing the impossibility when given the population
jokes.explanation: Good setup/payoff with classic Homer realization and catchphrase

jokes.text: Fine. I want to meet monkeys.
jokes.setup: Family debate about going to Brazil
jokes.punchline: Bart's simple, honest motivation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart moment - cutting through adult drama with childish honesty

jokes.text: It's really cheap if we change planes in Phoenix, Honolulu, Sun City and East Saint Louis, spend a night in a haunted house and leave right now.
jokes.punchline: Increasingly ridiculous travel requirements
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation of budget travel absurdities with haunted house twist

jokes.text: And I'll have been on every continent. Except Antarctica. The Simpsons are going to Antarctica next year. This year, Brazil.
jokes.punchline: Immediately planning another expensive trip
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's impulsive planning and grandiosity

jokes.text: Wait, wait, wait, wait, wait. So, in August, it's cold? That's right. And in February, it's hot? So, it's opposite land! Crooks chase cops, cats have puppies.
jokes.punchline: Homer's logic spiraling into complete absurdity
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer logic failure escalating to beautiful absurdist conclusions

jokes.text: No, Dad. It's just the weather. So, hot snow falls up? Yes. Woo-hoo!
jokes.setup: Lisa trying to correct Homer's misunderstanding
jokes.punchline: Lisa giving up and Homer celebrating nonsense
jokes.explanation: Great payoff to the opposite land bit with Lisa's resignation

jokes.text: Well done, Bart. But in Brazil, they speak Portuguese. Forget every word, boy. It's useless. But, Homer... I said forget it! All gone.
jokes.punchline: Homer demanding Bart forget Spanish entirely
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer parenting moment - wrong but decisive, absurdly authoritative

jokes.text: Fernando, you make that joke every time. It was that joke that made you fall in love with me.
jokes.punchline: Revealing the romantic history behind the recurring joke
jokes.explanation: Sweet meta-joke about recurring airline announcements with romantic backstory

jokes.text: Wow. It's like he's on the dashboard of the entire country.
jokes.punchline: Comparing religious icon to car dashboard ornament
jokes.explanation: Brilliant metaphor that's both irreverent and oddly apt, very quotable

jokes.text: Take me to the hotel / My hands are on a guy's ass / Boy, this dude must work out
jokes.setup: Homer joining a conga line for transportation
jokes.punchline: Homer's running commentary on intimate contact
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy with Homer's characteristic obliviousness to social norms

jokes.text: Look, Marge. I'm Brazilian!
jokes.punchline: Homer's instant cultural identification
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer immediate cultural assimilation based on one activity

jokes.text: Bert and Ernie left it to your imagination.
jokes.punchline: Comparing explicit content to Sesame Street
jokes.explanation: Sharp dark humor contrasting children's TV approaches across cultures

jokes.text: I'm Chiquita Banana and I'm here to say / I will eat this Toblerone and I will not pay
jokes.setup: Homer wearing fruit hat from minibar
jokes.punchline: Parodying jingle while admitting theft
jokes.explanation: Clever jingle parody with Homer's casual criminality

jokes.text: What a charming neighborhood. Mom, these are slums. The government just painted them bright colors so the tourists wouldn't be offended. Works for me.
jokes.punchline: Homer's satisfaction with superficial tourist deception
jokes.explanation: Sharp social commentary with Homer's willful ignorance as the punchline

jokes.text: There he is! Oh, wait. You said brown hair, right?
jokes.punchline: Homer's obvious racial confusion
jokes.explanation: Relies on racial stereotype humor - lazy writing

jokes.text: Ooh! They look like Skittles!
jokes.setup: Seeing colorful rats in the slums
jokes.punchline: Homer's candy comparison
jokes.explanation: Typical Homer finding the positive in obviously negative situations

jokes.text: Have you tried looking for him? That's plan B.
jokes.setup: Asking about missing orphan search efforts
jokes.punchline: Nun admitting they haven't tried the obvious solution
jokes.explanation: Excellent deadpan admission of institutional incompetence

jokes.text: Can you fly me over town to look for him? We nuns cannot fly. Oh, too much junk in the trunk, eh? Yes.
jokes.punchline: Nun matter-of-factly agreeing with crude weight comment
jokes.explanation: Absurd misunderstanding with unexpected nun self-deprecation

jokes.text: You know I'm a vegetarian. But you're on vacation, honey. I'm not wearing my wedding ring.
jokes.punchline: Homer's unrelated confession about marriage
jokes.explanation: Homer's vacation philosophy extending to marital obligations

jokes.text: Excuse me, Americans! How did you know?
jokes.punchline: Homer's obliviousness to how obvious they are
jokes.explanation: Good tourist stereotype humor with Homer's characteristic cluelessness

jokes.text: How did you know? There is a dress code on this beach. But we can help you.
jokes.punchline: Exploiting tourists with fake dress code
jokes.explanation: Standard tourist scam setup - functional but predictable

jokes.text: I feel so European. Huh. Mine keeps disappearing.
jokes.setup: Getting dressed for beach in skimpy swimwear
jokes.punchline: Homer's anatomy causing wardrobe malfunction
jokes.explanation: Standard body humor but well-executed with Marge's cultural comment

jokes.text: I'm in Rio / and I'm walking on the beach / I'm in my Speedo / No! No, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, no.
jokes.punchline: Bart's horrified reaction to seeing Homer in Speedo
jokes.explanation: Visual comedy based on Homer's inappropriate beachwear confidence

jokes.text: We are now developing our most powerful dance, the penetrada. It makes sex look like a church!
jokes.punchline: Extremely suggestive dance name and description
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly inappropriate cultural commentary with great wordplay

jokes.text: You can't protect her forever! You stupid lady!
jokes.punchline: Instructor's aggressive response and insult
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from cultural educator to hostile stranger

jokes.text: Ronaldo? You know him? No, no. I was just distracting you while my children robbed you.
jokes.punchline: Casual admission of orchestrated theft
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection joke with matter-of-fact criminal confession

jokes.text: My American friend, I'm afraid that this is a kidnapping. So that means I don't have to pay the fare? I suppose... Woo-hoo!
jokes.punchline: Homer's excitement about free ride
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - finding the silver lining in being kidnapped

jokes.text: No. It smells like cinnamon.
jokes.setup: Kidnapper telling Homer to remove bag from head
jokes.punchline: Homer's pleasant reason for keeping it on
jokes.explanation: Homer finding comfort in the strangest circumstances

jokes.text: I have a bladder the size of a Brazil nut. We just call them nuts here.
jokes.setup: Homer describing his small bladder with local reference
jokes.punchline: Kidnapper's correction about local naming convention
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay about geographical naming conventions

jokes.text: Clockwise! Counter-clockwise.
jokes.punchline: Extremely simple directional commands as entertainment
jokes.explanation: Absurdist children's programming that contrasts with earlier adult content

jokes.text: Kidnapped. Oh, my God! What are we supposed to do? I don't know. Wait for the call.
jokes.punchline: Lisa's matter-of-fact response about waiting
jokes.explanation: Lisa's unusual calm in crisis situation contrasts with Marge's panic

jokes.text: I don't think there is a boy or a husband! I think you have a thing for me. I've been shot!
jokes.punchline: Immediate consequence interrupting his theory
jokes.explanation: Good timing of violence interrupting inappropriate romantic advance

jokes.text: I don't know. They've been seeing me alive for free for a long time.
jokes.setup: Kidnappers wondering if family will pay ransom
jokes.punchline: Homer's economic analysis of his own value
jokes.explanation: Brilliant self-deprecating logic that's both funny and surprisingly insightful about family economics

jokes.text: That's very nice, honey. That'll buy you one of my legs or something they call a mystery bag.
jokes.punchline: Kidnapper's casual negotiation of body parts vs mysterious alternative
jokes.explanation: Dark humor with mysterious 'mystery bag' option adding absurdity

jokes.text: Hmm. Well, I'm high on sheep embryos, so I am feeling charitable.
jokes.setup: Homer asking Mr. Burns for ransom money
jokes.punchline: Burns' explanation for his generosity
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns moment - casual admission of bizarre drug use affecting his mood

jokes.text: Hey, Moe. Homer, listen. I need 50 grand. Don't ask me why. No, no. I need 50 grand! I asked you first! Fine. I'll send you 50 grand. Thanks.
jokes.punchline: Both needing money, Homer winning through persistence
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist logic with both characters needing money but Moe agreeing anyway

jokes.text: Go suck a Bible!
jokes.setup: Flanders offering prayers instead of money
jokes.punchline: Homer's religious-themed insult
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer insult that's both crude and religiously themed for Flanders

jokes.text: You cannot run from Carnival! Because even running is a kind of dance!
jokes.punchline: Performer's mystical carnival philosophy
jokes.explanation: Beautiful absurdist philosophy about the inescapability of carnival spirit

jokes.text: You cannot run from Carnival! Because even running is a kind of dance!
jokes.punchline: Impossible escape due to dance logic
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurd logic about inescapable cultural celebration

jokes.text: But I didn't know what state you lived in. It's a bit of a mystery, yes.
jokes.setup: Ronaldo explaining why he couldn't write
jokes.punchline: Lisa acknowledging the show's deliberately vague geography
jokes.explanation: Clever meta-joke about the show's intentionally ambiguous Springfield location

jokes.text: I make as much as Malcolm in the Middle. And because I have no parents, my earnings remain unstolen.
jokes.setup: Explaining how he can afford ransom money
jokes.punchline: Reference to child actor salaries and parental exploitation
jokes.explanation: Sharp commentary on child actor exploitation with dark twist about orphan benefits

jokes.text: Listen, I made a little scrapbook to remember the kidnapping. Look. This is that cigarette butt you burned me with. You slept like a baby that night.
jokes.punchline: Fond memories of being tortured, kidnapper's agreement
jokes.explanation: Perfect Stockholm syndrome portrayal with Homer's characteristic scrapbook hobby

jokes.text: He has the Stockholm syndrome. He has come to identify with his captors. They let me stay up all night!
jokes.punchline: Homer's childish reason for bonding with kidnappers
jokes.explanation: Excellent psychological diagnosis with Homer's simple pleasure as explanation

jokes.text: Look at all that pink and purple. Our money sure is gay.
jokes.punchline: Homer's casual observation about money's appearance
jokes.explanation: Simple observation humor that reflects the era's casual language use

jokes.text: It was Homer's idea. You say no to that face.
jokes.punchline: Even kidnapper can't resist Homer's appeal
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's weird charisma affecting even criminals

jokes.text: Because I've learned that no matter how bad I screw up, you'll always bail me out.
jokes.setup: Homer expressing what he learned from the experience
jokes.punchline: Wrong lesson that enables future bad behavior
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer learning the wrong lesson from consequences

jokes.text: Don't be sad. It's Carnival! Whoopee!
jokes.setup: Family falling off Sugar Loaf Mountain
jokes.punchline: Homer maintaining carnival enthusiasm while falling
jokes.explanation: Homer's relentless optimism even in dangerous situations

tmdb_overview: When Homer gets the family's telephone service cut off for refusing to pay for calls made to Rio de Janeiro, Brazil, Lisa confesses that she was the one who called Rio after sponsoring an orphan who goes missing. She convinces the family to travel to Brazil to look for him. However, once there, they have no luck finding him, and Homer is kidnapped.
